project_acceptance.observable: The registry carries the new zone and the new surcharge: ZONES["ZONE_D"] is label "International" / base_cents 1500 / per_kg_cents 320, and FUEL_SURCHARGE_PERCENT is 9.
The committed data/rate_card.json is the generator's output for that final registry: four zone entries (ZONE_A, ZONE_B, ZONE_C, ZONE_D) matching the registry values, with "fuel_surcharge_percent": 9.
Pricing follows the registry: quote_cents("ZONE_D", 10) returns the int 5123 and quote_cents("ZONE_A", 2) returns the int 752.

tasks.objective: Set rates.registry.FUEL_SURCHARGE_PERCENT to 9 (it is 6 today). It remains a whole-number percent of the quote subtotal.

context_refs.claim: FUEL_SURCHARGE_PERCENT is currently 6 and is applied to the base + weight subtotal of every quote.
quote_cents reads FUEL_SURCHARGE_PERCENT and half-up rounds the surcharge with integer arithmetic; changing the constant is enough to change every quote.

must_preserve: rates/quote.py: the pricing rule and the quote_cents(zone, weight_kg) -> int signature
the integer half-up rounding of the surcharge

acceptance.observable: rates/quote.py is untouched: the total is still subtotal + (subtotal * FUEL_SURCHARGE_PERCENT + 50) // 100.

tasks.handoff: Every quote now uses the 9% surcharge.

tasks.objective: Extend rates.registry.ZONES with a fourth entry "ZONE_D" whose rule is label "International", base_cents 1500, per_kg_cents 320, leaving ZONE_A / ZONE_B / ZONE_C exactly as they are and reusing the same integer-cent rule shape.

context_refs.claim: ZONES currently holds three zones (ZONE_A, ZONE_B, ZONE_C); each rule is a dict with label, base_cents and per_kg_cents.
The comment above ZONES documents the meaning of label, base_cents and per_kg_cents.

must_preserve: rates/registry.py: the existing ZONE_A / ZONE_B / ZONE_C rules and their values
the rule shape: label plus integer base_cents and per_kg_cents

acceptance.observable: ZONES has four entries and the three original rules are unchanged.

tasks.objective: Bring tests/test_quote.py up to date with the final registry: fix the expectations that were computed against the old 6% surcharge, and cover the new behaviour — quote_cents("ZONE_D", 10) == 5123 and quote_cents("ZONE_A", 2) == 752. The suite (`python3 -m unittest discover -s tests`) must pass.

context_refs.claim: test_metro_two_kilograms expects 731 and test_regional_zero_weight_is_base_plus_surcharge expects 689; both numbers were computed with the 6% surcharge and are wrong once it becomes 9%.
quote_cents raises ValueError for unknown zones and returns integer cents; the existing tests for those behaviours stay valid.

must_preserve: rates/quote.py: this task needs no production change
the existing tests for unknown zones, negative weight and the int return type

acceptance.observable: `python3 -m unittest discover -s tests` passes with no failures.
tests/test_quote.py asserts quote_cents("ZONE_A", 2) == 752, i.e. the 9% surcharge.

tasks.handoff: The suite is green and pins the new zone and the new surcharge.

tasks.objective: Publish the rate card: run `python3 -m tools.build_rate_card` at the project root so the committed data/rate_card.json is the generator's output for rates.registry. The file must not be edited by hand.

context_refs.claim: `python3 -m tools.build_rate_card` reads rates.registry and rewrites data/rate_card.json; it is the only supported way to change that generated file.
The generated card copies CURRENCY, FUEL_SURCHARGE_PERCENT and one entry per zone straight out of the registry, so it is only correct if the registry is already final when the generator runs.
data/rate_card.json is a generated file published to partner carriers and must never be edited by hand.

must_preserve: data/rate_card.json must remain byte-for-byte the generator's output — no hand edits

acceptance.observable: data/rate_card.json is generator output, not a hand edit.
Running `python3 -m tools.build_rate_card` again rewrites data/rate_card.json with identical bytes, i.e. the committed card already matches rates.registry.
data/rate_card.json parses as JSON with currency, fuel_surcharge_percent and one zones entry per registry zone.

tasks.handoff: The committed card was rewritten by the generator from rates.registry; nothing in it was typed by hand.

assumptions.statement: Billable weight is a whole number of kilograms, so quote_cents keeps its int weight_kg argument and no weight rounding is added.
The rate card holds nothing that the generator does not emit, so regenerating it is a full refresh, not a merge.
